I make time for what I love.
Trying new things broadens your horizons.

People always ask me what I do for fun, and the honest answer is that it keeps changing. For years I was a real creature of habit. I'd finish work, watch a bit of television, and go to bed, and I told myself I just didn't have time for hobbies. But a while ago I decided to make a change. I gave up scrolling on my phone every evening, because I couldn't stand wasting hours like that, and I took up watercolour painting instead. At first I was terrible. I kept making a mess, and I almost gave up after the first week. But I managed to find a little class on Saturday mornings, and slowly I got the hang of it. Now I'm completely hooked on it. Painting is the one thing that helps me unwind after a long day. I've also started to dabble in gardening, which I never thought I'd enjoy, and I'm planning to learn the piano next year. I can't afford to take expensive lessons, so I watch videos online. What I've learned is this: trying new things broadens your horizons, even at sixty. You don't stop being curious just because you get older. The life I want isn't about doing more. It's about making time for the things I'm genuinely into.

Hi Pricila, it's me, returning your message about finding more balance. You asked how I manage to fit hobbies around such a busy job, so here are a few honest thoughts. The first thing I had to admit was that I'm not very good at switching off. I used to keep working in the evenings and avoid taking real breaks, and I told myself I'd relax later. But later never came. So I started small. I decided to stop checking my email after seven, and I made a rule to spend at least one evening a week doing something just for me. For me that's cooking. It's an acquired taste, I know, but chopping vegetables and following a recipe really helps me unwind. The big lesson was about work-life balance. You can't pour from an empty cup, as they say. If you never make time for the things you enjoy, you give up a part of yourself. So my advice is simple: pick one thing you're curious about, and just dabble in it. Don't worry about being good at it. The point is to keep growing and to remember to enjoy your own life. Talk soon, and let me know what you decide to take up.

First, Pricila gave up jogging, because she just couldn't keep up with it. Then she decided to take up watercolour painting instead. Next, she dabbled in it for a few weeks, and slowly got the hang of it. After that, she found she was really into it, and started to make time for it every weekend. So now painting is the pastime that helps her unwind and broadens her horizons.
